SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims and provides empirical evidence (Tables 4–5, Fig.2) that the proposed linguistic regularizers improve LSTM/Bi-LSTM performance, and includes ablation studies. However, there are technical issues: the symmetric KL definition in Eq. (6) is written as DKL(p||q) = 1/2 sum p(l) log q(l) + q(l) log p(l), which is not the standard symmetric KL form and appears incorrect. Some modeling choices are underspecified or unusual (e.g., the claim that forward and backward LSTM parameters are shared) and training/ statistical details (random seeds, variance, significance) are not provided, limiting reproducibility and confidence in correctness.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The idea of incorporating sentiment lexicons and modeling negation/intensifiers is not new (the paper itself cites Teng et al., Zhu et al., and prior lexicon/negation work), but the paper's approach—adding explicit regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) to LSTM/Bi-LSTM and learning shifting distributions/transform matrices—is a non-trivial combination and engineering contribution. Thus the work is an incremental but meaningful extension rather than a wholly novel paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: The paper follows a standard organization (related work, method, experiments) and many concepts are motivated (Sections 3–4, and discussion in 4.6). However, the provided text contains repeated and garbled paragraphs, some missing formulas (intensity regularizer formulas omitted) and notation/claims that are not fully explained (e.g., parameter sharing in Bi-LSTM), which reduces clarity and makes some parts hard to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments include ablation studies (Table 4), focused subset evaluations for negation/intensifiers (Table 5), and analysis of intensity effects (Fig.2), which address several important aspects. Still, important reproducibility details (training hyperparameters, statistical significance, dataset splits) and some methodological specifics (exact form of intensity regularizer, how lexicon classes are assigned) are omitted in the provided text, limiting thoroughness.

IMPACT.justification: Modeling linguistic phenomena (negation, intensity, lexicons) for sentence-level sentiment is an important and practical problem and the paper shows improvements on common benchmarks (MR, SST), so the contribution is of moderate practical interest. The approach is incremental and unlikely to be transformative, but could inform follow-up work that integrates linguistic priors into neural models.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—improving neural sentiment classification by integrating linguistic regularizers—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The paper compares to standard baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN) and evaluates on canonical datasets (MR, SST), which is appropriate for such venues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper lists and (in the experiments) compares to a broad set of relevant baselines (RNN/RNTN, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL) and performs ablation studies isolating each regularizer (Tables 4–5). However, the excerpt does not show detailed experimental settings to confirm full fairness (e.g., identical hyperparameters, significance testing), so while comparisons are reasonable they lack some rigor in the provided text.
